commands.repair_reason: - [P1] No automated code repair is needed; the remaining merge blocker is contributor or maintainer real behavior proof for the performance path.; Cleared: The diff only changes Swift menu-refresh code and tests; it does not touch dependencies, CI, secrets, signing, publishing, or credential handling. commands.review_summary:
The PR skips closed-menu readiness-signature computation, resyncs the baseline on root menu open, switches double signature formatting to bit patterns, and adds a focused regression test.

Reproducibility: no. high-confidence live reproduction was established in this review. Source inspection confirms the current-main hot path computes the readiness signature before invalidateMenus can use the open-menu state, and the PR adds a focused stale-baseline regression test.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] No automated code repair is needed; the remaining merge blocker is contributor or maintainer real behavior proof for the performance path.

target.title: perf: cut menu readiness signature cost on store changes
target.body:
## Summary

Targets the popup-menu lag reported in #1321. Multiple users confirmed the lag appeared in the 0.32 series and that downgrading to 0.31.0 restores responsiveness, so this focuses on a 0.32-era main-thread regression that still exists on `main`.

The menu *readiness signature* (`menuAdjunctReadinessSignature()`) serializes every enabled provider's token snapshot plus its 30-day daily breakdown (and the OpenAI dashboard per-service breakdown) into a string on **every** store mutation, via `observeStoreChanges`. This runs on the main actor on each background refresh tick and scales with provider/account count.

Three changes:

- **Skip the signature entirely when no menu is open.** `observeStoreChanges` always computed `didMenuAdjunctReadinessChange()` to pass as `refreshOpenMenus`, but `invalidateMenus` only consults that argument while a menu is open. When the menu is closed (the common case during background refresh) the result was computed and discarded.
- **Re-anchor the baseline when a root menu opens (correctness for the skip).** Because the baseline is no longer recomputed on every closed-menu store change, it can drift from live store data. Without re-anchoring, a menu reopened from new data followed by an open-menu change that *reverts* to the previous baseline value would compare equal and skip the rebuild, leaving stale content visible. The baseline is now resynced when a **root** menu opens (it is rebuilt from current data at that point); nested submenu opens intentionally do **not** resync, so a pending parent refresh can't be masked. Thanks to @clawsweeper for catching this.
- **Make the signature cheaper.** Replace `String(format: "%.8f", …)` (a hot per-value cost run for every daily/service value of every provider) with the raw `Double` bit pattern. The signature is only ever compared for equality against the previous signature, so the bit pattern is both exact (no rounding collisions) and far cheaper. This also helps while a menu *is* open, where the signature is recomputed on each tick.

Net effect: less main-thread work both at idle (closed) and during interaction (open), which is the responsiveness path that regressed in 0.32.

## Proof

**Runtime cost of the work this PR skips when closed**, measured on the real `menuAdjunctReadinessSignature()` with a realistic multi-provider store (4 providers enabled, each with a 30-day token daily breakdown, 5000 calls, warm):

```
menuAdjunctReadinessSignature(): 0.391s / 5000 = ~78 us/call
```

That full ~78 µs call ran on the **main actor on every store mutation pre-fix, even with all menus closed** (i.e. on each background refresh tick). It now runs only when a menu is actually open. The cost grows with enabled providers/accounts, matching the multi-provider setups in #1321/#1325.

**Why the per-call cost itself also dropped — signature double-formatting micro-benchmark** (`swiftc -O`, 60 doubles/iter × 200k iters):

```
old(%.8f):       10.799s
new(bitPattern):  1.166s
speedup: 9.3x
```

**Regression test is negative-validated.** The new `reopening root menu resyncs readiness baseline so reverted store data still refreshes` test was confirmed to **fail** when the resync is disabled and **pass** with it, so it genuinely guards the staleness path:

```
# resync disabled:
✘ Expectation failed: controller.didMenuAdjunctReadinessChange()
# resync enabled:
✔ Test "reopening root menu resyncs readiness baseline …" passed
```

(Still open: maintainer/reporter real-machine confirmation on the multi-provider/multi-account setup from #1321 — I don't have that exact configuration locally. The change is logically a strict reduction of main-thread work on the documented hot path.)

## Test plan

- [x] `swift build`
- [x] `make check` (SwiftFormat + SwiftLint, 0 violations, 1018 files)
- [x] `swift test --filter StatusMenuTests` (81 tests) — includes the existing "data arriving after open rebuilds the parent menu" coverage plus the new baseline-resync regression test
- [x] Negative validation of the new regression test (fails without the fix)
- [x] Runtime measurement of the skipped signature call + the formatting speedup (see Proof)
- [ ] Maintainer/reporter real-machine confirmation with multiple providers/accounts

## Notes / follow-ups (not in this PR)

- Chart submenus still `removeAllItems` + rebuild their `NSHostingView` when underlying data changes; gating that on a per-provider content fingerprint is a possible follow-up (overlaps active menu-card-height work).
- The multi-account "freeze on open" reports appear largely mitigated on current `main` by the recent Codex token-scan budget commits.

Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)
target.author: yuxin-qiao
